Therefore, the relaxed phase of U(1) loc q-1 gauge theories is compatible with vortices (magnetic monopoles) at finite temperature. Note that for ordinary U(1) 0 superfluids, there is no notion of the temporal-Goldstone ϕ, so the restriction is still the same as it was at zero temperature, i.e. all the dependence on the U(1) 0 phase φ must arise via ξ to allow for vortices in the configurations of φ.
Due to the presence of vortices, the equilibrium action in eq. (4.18) can now contain a new term
div.formula: S eqb = Σ β χ 2 µ ∧ * µ - f s 2 ξ ∧ * ξ + χ 2 µ ∧ * µ - 1/ χ 2 dV ∧ * dV -ι u (ξ ∧ Ã) -˜ (-1) q ι u (dV ∧ Φ). (4.22)
div.p: We have also introduced a source term coupled to dV in the equilibrium action. The equilibrium versions of U(1) q , U(1) p currents and the U(1) q-1 defect current are the same as eq. (4.15) and (4.11) respectively. However, we now have a U(1) p-1 defect current
div.formula: Leqb = -u ∧ * dV = (-) q dV . (4.23)
div.p: Varying this with respect to the time-components of φ, we recover the Gauss constraint in eq. (4.19). However, varying with respect to the spatial components of φ and V , we now find

For any nonzero ˜ , the first equation is just the derivative of the second. This is to be expected on account of the U(1) loc q spatial gauge symmetry remaining after the gaugefixing in eq. (4.21). On the other hand, for ˜ = 0, the second equation becomes trivial, while the first reverts to the original vortex-free version of the configuration equations in eq. (4.16b). The Bianchi identity in the presence of vortices becomes eq. (3.29), while the Bianchi identity (4.12b) remains the same.

The q-form charges and defects are screened as in eq. (4.13). In the presence of vortices, the spatial components of ξ are also relaxed as we can see from eq. (4.24b). In the absence of background fields, this yields
where k0 = ˜ √ χ f s denotes the new inverse correlation length associated with the spatial components of ξ.

div.head: Duality transformations
div.p: As we have discussed at length in section 2, when the U(1) q global symmetry is not explicitly broken, we can employ the duality procedure in section 3.1 to dress the theory of U(1) q superfluids with vortices equivalently as a U(1) p pseudo-superfluid in the relaxed phase. The ingredients we need are the dual-Goldstone terms similar to eq. (3.14) to impose the Bianchi identities
div.formula: S eqb ∼ Σ β ι u 1 c φ d φ ∧ (ξ -c φ A -˜ V ) + ι u ˜ c φ (-) p+1 d φ ∧ dV . (4.26)
div.p: We can now go ahead and integrate out ξ and dV to give
div.formula: ξ = (-) pq+p+q 1 f s * μ + (-) p+1 c φ χ u ∧ * ξ,(4.27a
div.p: where μ, μ are defined using φ = ι β φ/c φ , φ = ι β φ/c φ similar to eqs. (4.4) and (4.9). Substituting these back into the equilibrium action together with the Lagrange multiplier terms in eq. ( 4.26), we recover the relaxed phase of a U(1) p pseudo-superfluid without vortices in eq. (4.18), with the same substitutions outlined in eqs. (3.16) and (3.36) together with
where we have identified χ = 1/f s .

Normally, at zero temperature, we cannot perform a duality transformation when the U(1) q symmetry is explicitly broken. This is because the description now explicitly depends on the Goldstone field φ and not on its derivatives ξ alone. However, when describing the relaxed phase of a U(1) q superfluid in thermal equilibrium, the transformation (4.17) implies that equilibrium action only explicitly depends on the time-component ϕ = ι β φ/c φ of the Goldstone field φ and not on its spatial components. Therefore, we still have access to the partial duality transformation, where we only integrate out the spatial components of ξ. To this end, instead of eq. (4.26), we only introduce a (p -1)-form Lagrange multiplier φ and a (p -2)-form Lagrange multiplier φ for the spatial components of the Bianchi identities, i.e.
div.formula: S eqb ∼ -(-) pq+p+q T 0 Σ β d φ ∧ (ξ -c φ A -˜ V ) -(-) p ˜ d φ ∧ dV. (4.29)
div.p: The configuration equations for the spatial components of ξ and dV read
div.formula: P u ξ = 1 f s (-) pq+p+q * μ, P u dV = -(-) pq+q χ * μ . (4.30)
div.p: We can use this, together with the time-components of the two objects ι u ξ = c φ µ, ι u dV = 0, to find an equivalent form of the equilibrium action
div.formula: S eqb = Σ β χ 2 µ ∧ * µ + χ 2 μ ∧ * μ + χ 2 µ ∧ * µ + χ 2 μ ∧ * μ + c φ T F ∧ φ -µ ∧ Ã . (4.31)
div.p: Interestingly, we observe that this representation corresponds to a system with a temporally-spontaneously broken approximate U(1) q × U(1) p symmetry, with a mixed anomaly between the two symmetry groups. The terms in the last line are precisely the anomalous contribution to the equilibrium action that can be treated by using the bulk anomaly-inflow action; see eq. (B.8).
Varying the full equilibrium action with respect to the background sources, we can read out the respective currents
div.formula: J eqb = u ∧ n -cφ * μ, Jeqb = u ∧ ñ -c φ * µ, L eqb = u ∧ n , Leqb = u ∧ ñ ,(4.32)
div.p: where we have further identified ñ = χμ and ñ = χ μ . We can extremise the equilibrium action with respect to ϕ and φ to recover the anomalous Gauss constraints
together with the Bianchi identities dµ = -ι u F, (4.33c)
div.formula: dμ = -ι u F (4.33d) dµ = (µ -ι u Ξ) ,(4.33e
dμ = ˜ μ -ι u Ξ . (4.33f)
div.p: The first and fifth of these equations (4.33a) and (4.33e) are just the original U(1) q Gauss constraint in eq. ( 4. 19) and the Bianchi identity in eq. (4.12b). The second and third equations (4.33b) and (4.33c) implement the Bianchi identities (3.29). Finally, the forth and sixth equations (4.33d) and (4.33f) implement eq. (4.24a) and (4.24b) respectively.
Due to the final two Bianchi identities, all charges are screened as
The first two equations are precisely eq. (4.13), whereas the last two are a consequence of eq. (4.25).

This representation of the theory is neat because it is symmetric in the U(1) q and U(1) p sectors. It also allows us to infer that a the relaxed phase of a U(1) q pseudosuperfluid with vortices is dual to the relaxed phase of a U(1) p pseudo-superfluid with vortices. Under this duality, all "tilde" quantities get exchanged with the respective "untilde" quantities.

div.head: Applications
div.p: Superfluids with vortices: To summarise, the model above describes a theory of relaxed U(1) q or U(1) p superfluids in the presence of vortices. If we increase the susceptibility of vortices by increasing ˜ , thereby decreasing the correlation length 1/ k0 , we can integrate out the spatial components of the superfluid velocity χμ = (-) q * ξ from the setup altogether using eq. (4.34). This leads us to the theory of relaxed fluids with temporally-spontaneously broken U(1) q global symmetry discussed in section 4.1. In this sense, the proliferation of vortices controls the phase transition from superfluids to fluids. Analogously, by increasing the susceptibility of U(1) q-1 defects by increasing , thereby decreasing the correlation length 1/k 0 , we can integrate out the q-form chemical potential χµ = (-) p * ξ from the setup using eq. (4.34). This results in a theory of just dynamical superfluid velocity, furnishing a temporally-spontaneously broken U(1) p global symmetry in section 4.1; see section 4.1.3. These notions of phase transitions will be made more precise in the subsequent hydrodynamic discussion.

div.head: Electromagnetism with free electric and magnetic charges:
div.p: The setup outlined above can also be understood as describing the relaxed phase of a U(1) loc q-1 or U(1) loc p-1 gauge theory in thermal equilibrium, in the presence of free electric and possibly magnetic charges. Taking the U(1) loc q-1 interpretation, we can identify -c φ µ as electric field, χμ as magnetic fields, χ/c 2 φ as the electric permittivity, χ as the magnetic permeability. Furthermore, we can identify -χ µ /c φ as the electric charge density and ˜ χ μ as the magnetic charge density, with 2 χ /c 2 φ and ˜ 2 χ their respective susceptibilities.

Therefore, in the presence of free electric charges, electric fields and electric charges themselves are screened to a finite Debye length 1/k 0 . If we proliferate the free electric charges by increasing , thereby decreasing the electric Debye screening length 1/k 0 , we can integrate out electric fields and free electric charges from the description using the equations above. This leaves us with the theory of magnetohydrodynamics with magnetic monopoles, with an approximate U(1) p symmetry detailed in section 4.1; see section 4.1.3. Similarly, in the presence of free magnetic charges/monopoles, magnetic field and magnetic charges are screened to a finite magnetic Debye length 1/ k0 . If we wish, we can identically switch off the effects of magnetic monopoles by setting ˜ to zero, which takes k0 → 0 and unscreens the magnetic fields. On the other hand, if we proliferate the magnetic monopoles by increasing ˜ , thereby decreasing the magnetic Debye screening length 1/ k0 , we can integrate out magnetic fields and free magnetic charges from the description. In this case, we are left with the theory of electrohydrodynamics with free electric charges, with an approximate U(1) q symmetry detailed in section 4.1.

div.head: Pseudo-spontaneous symmetry breaking and pinned pseudo-superfluids
div.p: Let us switch gears and consider the pinned phase of a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid, where the topological U(1) q-1 global symmetry is completely spontaneously broken, giving mass to the q-form pseudo-Goldstone field φ. The resultant model also describes a U(1) loc q-1 gauge theory in the Higgs phase, where the U(1) loc q-1 gauge symmetry is spontaneously broken and the dynamical q-form gauge field φ acquires a mass. For q = 0, this model describes a pinned U(1) 0 superfluid with a massive 0-form pseudo-Goldstone field φ, while for q = 1 it describes a U(1) 0 superconductor with a massive dynamical 1-form gauge field φ. We will talk more about these applications after setting up the model.

div.head: Pseudo-spontaneous symmetry breaking
div.p: Since both the U(1) q and U(1) q-1 global symmetries are completely spontaneously broken, we have access to the full Goldstone fields φ and φ . Using these, we can construct the completely gauge-invariant misalignment tensor ψ defined in eq. (3.38). One can check that we still have ι u ψ = -c φ µ , so this definition of ψ only differs from its relaxed phase definition in eq. (4.17) by spatial terms. The new gauge-invariant information in ψ allows us to introduce a new mass term for ψ in the equilibrium action (4.18) as
div.formula: S eqb = Σ β χ 2 µ ∧ * µ - f s 2 ξ ∧ * ξ + χ 2 µ ∧ * µ - m 2 2 ψ ∧ * ψ -ι u (ξ ∧ Ã) + (-) q ι u (ψ ∧ Ãψ ) . (4.35)
div.p: Here m is the pinning mass that we also encountered previously in section 3.2. We have also included the Ãψ coupling term for ψ from eq. (3.39). The U(1) q charge current is still given by eq. (4.15), while the defect current can be obtained by varying the equilibrium action, together with the bulk action in eq. (3.45), with respect to Φ, to yield
div.formula: L eqb = χ u ∧ µ + c φ m 2 P u ψ = χ -c 2 φ m 2 u ∧ µ + c φ m 2 ψ. (4.36)
div.p: The configuration equations for the time-component of φ give rise to the same Gauss constraint in eq. (4. 19), but with the renewed definition of F in eq. (3.42). However, the configuration equations coming from the spatial components of φ modify from eq. (4.16b) to
div.formula: d * ξ = (-) q m 2 f s * ψ -(-) q 1 f s ι u F . (4.37)
div.p: The Bianchi identities associated with ψ is given by eq. ( 3.40) and that associated with ξ is given in eq. (3.6b). Since φ acquires a mass, the temporal as well as spatial components of the superfluid velocity ξ and ψ are screened.
The screening of the temporal components c φ µ = ι u ξ and c φ µ = -ι u ψ is still given by eq. (4.13), however the screening of the spatial components of ξ and ψ takes the form

where k φ 0 = m/ √ f s denotes the finite inverse correlation length of φ due to pinning. Recall that ξ was also screened in the presence of vortices in eq. (4.25). However, the precise form of the screening equations are different in the two cases.

div.head: Duality transformations
div.p: As we discussed in section 3.3, the pinned phase of a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid is self-dual under q ↔ p + 1. To see the finite temperature realisation of this duality, we need to introduce the Lagrange multiplier φ and φψ to impose the Bianchi identities
div.formula: S eqb ∼ Σ β ι u 1 c φ d φ ∧ (ξ -c φ A) + ι u 1 c φ φψ ∧ (dψ -ξ + c φ Ξ) . (4.39)
div.p: Together with the equilibrium action eq. ( 4.35), this can be used to integrate out ξ and ψ from the theory to give
div.formula: ψ = (-) pq+q m 2 * μ ψ + c φ χ u ∧ * ξψ ,(4.40b)
div.p: where the new definition of ξ is being used from eq. (3.55) in the pinned phase. The dual chemical potentials, on the other hand, are defined as
div.formula: μψ T 0 = ι β Ãψ -d φψ , (4.41a) μ T 0 = ι β Ã -d φ -φψ ,(4.41b)
div.p: where φ = ι β φ/c φ and φψ = ι β φψ /c φ . Substituting these back, we obtain back the same functional form of the equilibrium action with the mappings in eq. (3.56) together with

We can also derive an intermediate picture purely in terms of temporally-spontaneously broken higher-form symmetry by performing a partial duality transformation. To this end, we only introduce the temporal components of the Lagrange multipliers to impose the spatial components of the Bianchi identities, i.e.
div.formula: S eqb ∼ -(-) pq+p+q T 0 Σ β d φ ∧ (ξ -c φ A) -φψ ∧ (dψ -ξ + c φ Ξ) . (4.43)
div.p: This still allows us to integrate out the spatial components of ξ and ψ as
div.formula: P u ξ = (-) pq+p+q 1 f s * μ,(4.44a)
P u ψ = (-) pq+q m 2 * μ ψ .(4.44b)
div.p: Further using ι u ξ = c φ µ and ι u ψ = -χ φ µ , we can substitute these expressions into the onshell action to obtain a dual form
div.formula: S eqb = Σ β χ 2 µ ∧ * µ + 1 2f s μ ∧ * μ + χ 2 µ ∧ * µ + 1 2m 2 μψ ∧ * μ ψ + c φ T 0 F ∧ φ -µ ∧ Ã + c φ (-) q T 0 Ξ ∧ φψ -µ ∧ Ãψ . (4.45)
div.p: The anomalous terms in the last two lines of this expression can be removed by using the bulk anomaly-inflow action; see eq. (B.14). The remaining terms in the dual form manifest the q ↔ p + 1 symmetry of the pinned phase of a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid.
Varying the full action with respect to the background sources, we can read off the respective currents
div.formula: J eqb = u ∧ n -cφ * μ, Jeqb = u ∧ ñ -c φ * µ, L eqb = u ∧ n + (-) p cφ * μ ψ , Jψ,eqb = u ∧ ñψ -(-) q c φ * µ . (4.46)
div.p: The anomalous Gauss constraints take the similar form
div.formula: d * n = c φ P u F + (-) q+1 * n , d * ñ ψ = (-) p+1 cφ P u Ξ + (-) p * ñ,(4.47a)
div.p: together with the Bianchi identities
div.formula: dµ = -ι u F,(4.47b
dμ ψ = -ι u Fψ , (4.47c) dµ = µ -ι u Ξ, (4.47d) dμ = μψ -ι u F . (4.47e)
div.p: All charged objects are also screened in the pinned phase.
But the actual form of the relaxation is different and is given as
These are just rewriting of the equations found the respective equations found previously.

div.head: Superconductivity:
div.p: The above construction applies to the spontaneously broken phase of a U(1) loc q-1 gauge theory, with massive gauge fields ψ. For q = 1, this describes the theory of superconductivity with a spontaneouslybroken U(1) 0 gauge symmetry. The massive gauge fields ξ can only propagate over a finite correlation length 1/k φ 0 , identified as the London penetration depth in a superconductor.

div.head: HIGHER-FORM PSEUDO-HYDRODYNAMICS
div.p: In this section, we discuss the theory of hydrodynamics with an approximate higher-form symmetry in the temporally-spontaneously broken phase. The setup we employ here is a direct generalisation of our recent work on approximate 0-form symmetries [8] and utilises the hydrodynamic framework for higher-form symmetries from [5,22]. We will assume relativistic symmetry through this discussion. The generalisation to Galilean systems or systems without any boost symmetry is involved but conceptually straight-forward, and we will consider it in a future publication.

div.head: Hydrodynamic fields and equations
div.p: When we leave thermal equilibrium, we no longer have the luxury to base our effective description on an equilibrium action like in section 4.1. We must instead rely on the framework of hydrodynamics, where the starting point are symmetries and the respective conservation laws. The rationale being that out-of-equilibrium processes are generically dissipative and the only long-lived modes that are relevant for the low-energy effective description are those corresponding to conserved quantities. As we have discussed in detail in the previous subsections, for a system respecting an approximate U(1) q global symmetry, the relevant conservation law is
(5.1a) Furthermore, we assume the system to be thermodynamically isolated and invariant under spacetime translations, in which case we also have an energy-momentum conservation equation
div.formula: ∇ µ T µν = (F • J) ν + ( Ξ • L) ν , (5.1b)
div.p: where T µν is the symmetric energy-momentum tensor and ∇ µ denotes the covariant derivative operator associated with the background spacetime metric g µν . We have used the notation (F • J) ν = 1/(q + 1)! F νλ1... J λ1... and similarly for the second term on the right. The force terms sourcing the energy-momentum conservation are a direct generalisation of the Lorentz force for 0-form symmetries. Depending on the particular application in mind, one might also need to consider additional conservation equations associated with particle number, etc. We will ignore these for now for simplicity.

To solve these hydrodynamic equations, we need the appropriate degrees of freedom. Following our previous work on 1-form hydrodynamics [5], we note that the hydrodynamic fields can be identified as a set of symmetry parameters corresponding to the global symmetries of the system under consideration. For our case of interest, these are a q-form parameter Λ β and a diffeomorphism parameter β µ = u µ /T , with u µ being the normalised timelike fluid velocity (with u µ u µ = -1) and T the fluid temperature. These fields transform under infinitesimal diffeomorphisms χ µ and U(1) q transformations Λ as
div.formula: β µ → β µ + £ χ β µ , (5.2a) Λ β → Λ β + £ χ Λ β -£ β Λ.
div.p: When the U(1) q global symmetry is explicitly broken, for q > 0, we also have a new global U(1) q-1 symmetry associated with the conservation of defects. Correspondingly, we introduce a (q -1)-form parameter Λ β transforming under diffeomorphisms χ µ and (q -1)-form gauge transformations Λ as
div.formula: Λ β → Λ β + £ χ Λ β -£ β Λ . (5.2c)
div.p: In equilibrium, the hydrodynamic fields take trivial values β µ = δ µ t /T 0 and Λ β = Λ β = 0. Using eq. ( 5.2), we can see that these remain unchanged under time-independent diffeomorphisms and gauge transformations.

As we discussed in section 4.1, we need to temporallyspontaneously break the U(1) q and U(1) q-1 global symmetries of the theory, for q > 0 and q > 1 respectively, to allow for non-trivial chemical potentials. This gives rise to temporal-Goldstone fields ϕ and ϕ , satisfying ι β ϕ = ι β ϕ = 0, and transforming as
div.formula: ϕ → ϕ + £ χ ϕ -ι β Λ, (5.3a) ϕ → ϕ + £ χ ϕ -ι β Λ .
div.p: We will also need Josephson equations to govern the timeevolution of these temporal-Goldstones, which take the usual form
div.formula: £ β ϕ = ι β Λ β ,(5.4a)
£ β ϕ = ι β Λ β .
div.p: The Λ β and Λ β terms on the right are necessitated by symmetries. In principle, these equations can admit derivative corrections. However, by judiciously redefining the time-components of Λ β and Λ β , we can absorb all such corrections and impose the above equations exactly.

An astute reader will notice the mismatch between the equations of motion and the degrees of freedom. The two Josephson equations determine the evolution of ϕ and ϕ , while the two conservation equations determine the dynamics of β µ and Λ β . However, there is no independent equation of motion for the field Λ β . This can be pinned down to a secret gauge symmetry in the theory. Consider a U(1) q transformation with parameter Λ = dλ, together with a U(1) q-1 transformation with parameter Λ = λ. This leaves both the background fields A and Φ invariant, but instead acts as a U(1) loc q-1 local gauge transformation on the dynamical fields of the theory
div.formula: Λ β → Λ β -£ β dλ, Λ β → Λ β -£ β λ, ϕ → ϕ -ι β dλ, ϕ → ϕ -ι β λ. (5.5)
div.p: This gauge symmetry accounts for the additional unphysical degrees of freedom in the description. We could fix a gauge to try and rid ourselves of the unphysical degrees of freedom, but it is cleaner to play along and keep the gauge symmetry manifest for the moment.
We can use the dynamical field content of the theory to define the gauge-invariant U(1) q chemical potential µ and the U(1) q-1 defect chemical potential µ via µ T = Λ β + ι β A -dϕ, (5.6a)
div.formula: µ T = Λ β + ι β Φ -dϕ -ϕ .
div.p: The Josephson equations (5.4) imply that both of these chemical potentials are purely spatial
div.formula: ι β µ = ι β µ = 0,(5.7)
div.p: as we obtained in equilibrium. Together, β µ , µ, and µ make up the right amount of degrees of freedom to solve for using the hydrodynamic equations (5.1).

Before we proceed, it is important to set a derivative counting scheme. To keep the effects of explicit symmetry breaking small in the effective theory, we take the symmetry breaking parameter to be of ∼ O(∂). We choose the usual derivative-scaling for the gauge field A ∼ O(∂ 0 ), while for the dynamical and background phase fields we choose ϕ, Φ ∼ O(∂ -1 ), so that µ and µ are O(∂ 0 ). This is the same derivative counting we employed in our recent work on approximate 0-form symmetries [8].

div.head: Second law of thermodynamics and constitutive relations
div.p: An important aspect of the hydrodynamic framework is the local second law of thermodynamics. As such, all we need to complete the hydrodynamic equations is a set of constitutive relations for the currents J, L, T µν in terms of the hydrodynamic variables u µ , T , µ, and µ (for q > 0), along with the background field strengths F and Ξ. However, the second law of thermodynamics imposes constraints on the constitutive relations as it postulates the existence of an entropy current S µ whose divergence is locally positive semi-definite
for all possible solutions of the hydrodynamic equations.
In practise, it is useful to work with the free energy current N µ defined as
div.formula: T N µ = T S µ + T µν u ν + (J • µ) µ + (L • µ ) µ .
div.p: In terms of this, the statement of the second law turns into the so-called adiabaticity equation
div.formula: ∇ µ N µ = 1 2 T µν δ B g µν + J • δ B A + L • δ B Φ + ∆, (5.10)
div.p: for some positive-semi-definite quadratic form ∆ representing entropy production. To compactify the notation, we have utilised the variations of the background fields along the hydrodynamic data
div.formula: δ B g µν = £ β g µν = 2∇ (µ β ν) , δ B A = £ β A + dΛ β = d µ T + ι β F, δ B Φ = £ β Φ -Λ β + dΛ β = d µ T + ι β Ξ - µ T . (5.11)
div.p: The primary benefit of the adiabaticity equation over the statement of the second law is that it is satisfied even off-shell, i.e. even when the hydrodynamic conservation equations are not necessarily imposed.

div.head: Constitutive relations
div.p: The adiabaticity equation can be used to classify the hydrodynamic constitutive relations consistent with the second law of thermodynamics to arbitrary orders in derivatives [23][24][25]. We will not perform this exercise here. Instead, for illustrative purposes, we focus just on "canonical transport", characterised by a simple free energy current
div.formula: N µ = P β µ + N µ .
div.p: Here P denotes the thermodynamic pressure that generically depends on all the zero-derivative order Lorentz scalars in the theory constructed out T , µ, and µ . The vector N µ denotes possible non-canonical corrections to the free energy current. Since the higher-form chemical potentials carry Lorentz indices, the number of such scalars crucially depends on the rank q of the higher-form symmetry and the number of spatial dimensions d. 14 We can sweep this complication under the rug for now by pretending that P is a function of the thermodynamic parameters directly and expressing its derivatives as
div.formula: dP = sδT + n • δµ + n • δµ - 1 2
div.p: r µν δg µν .

The cost we pay is that the differential of P now also explicitly depends on the differential of the spacetime metric used for the contraction of indices. We can identify s as the thermodynamic entropy density, n as the q-form charge density, n as the (q -1)-form defect density, and r µν the anisotropic stress tensor. We can also define the thermodynamic energy density

Since P is a scalar, it cannot arbitrarily depend on the metric and hence r µν is not independent. In fact, requiring the Lie derivative of P to be given by the Lie derivatives of its constituents, we can find that

14 For q = 0 and any d, we only have T and µ as we expect from ordinary 0-form hydrodynamics. For q = 1 and any d, we have T and µµµ µ , but also µv because the defects themselves furnish a 0-form symmetry. For higher-q, the counting becomes dimension dependent. For instance for q = 2, d = 3, we have T , µµν µ µν , µ v µ µ µ v , and the product µµν µ νρ µ v ρ µ µ v . But for q = 2, d ≥ 4, we also have independent chemical potential chains like µµν µ νρ µρσµ σµ .

The condition in the last line can be understood as a constraint on the densities due to the Lorentz invariance. In the simplified case when the pressure only depends on T , µ 2 , and µ 2 , we find that n = χµ, n = χ µ , (5.15) where χ and χ are susceptibilities of q-form charge and (q -1)-form defects respectively. One can check that the Lorentz-invariance constraint is trivially satisfied.
Let us now move on to the constitutive relations. Using the fact that ∇ µ (P β µ ) = 1 2 P g µν δ B g µν + δ B P into the adiabaticity equation (5.10), and using
div.formula: δ B T = T 2 u µ u ν δ B g µν , δ B µ = µ 2 u µ u ν δ B g µν + ι u δ B A, δ B µ = µ 2 u µ u ν δ B g µν + ι u δ B Φ ,(5.16)
div.p: we can read off the constitutive relations
div.formula: T µν = ( + P )u µ u ν + P g µν -r µν + T µν , J = u ∧ n + J , L = u ∧ n + L .
div.p: Here T µν , J , L denote the respective dissipative corrections, which we will return to below. These constitutive relations satisfy the adiabaticity equation in the absence of dissipative corrections.

We can use the second law of thermodynamics to constrain the derivative corrections that can appear in the hydrodynamic constitutive relations. Plugging the constitutive relations (5.17) into the adiabaticity equation (5.10), we can derive a constraint equation to be satisfied by the derivative corrections
div.formula: - 1 2 T µν δ B g µν -J • δ B A -L • δ B Φ + ∇ µ N µ = ∆ ≥ 0. (5.18)
div.p: We already fixed the redefinition freedom in the timecomponent of Λ β to set ι β µ = 0. Further, using the redefinition freedom in the spatial components of Λ β , along with those in β µ and ϕ, we can choose these dissipative corrections to satisfy the Landau frame condition
div.formula: T µν u ν = ι u J = ι u L = 0.

div.head: Dissipative corrections
div.p: For simplicity, we shall ignore any non-canonical transport characterised by N µ and further focus only on fluids enjoying parity and charge-conjugation invariance; see table I for discrete transformation properties of various fields. The most general such derivative corrections up to first order in derivatives are given as
div.formula: T µν = -T ηP µρ P νσ δ B g ρσ - T 2 ζ -2 d η P µν P ρσ δ B g ρσ = -2ηP λ(µ ∇ ρ u ν) -ζ -2 d η P µν ∇ λ u λ , J = -T σ P u δ B A = -σ T P u d µ T + ι u F , L = -T σ P u δ B Φ = -σ T P u d µ T + (ι u Ξ -µ) ,(5.20)
div.p: where ∆ µν = g µν + u µ u ν is the projector transverse to the fluid velocity. The shear viscosity η, bulk viscosity ζ, conductivity σ, and the defect conductivity σ are all non-negative dissipative transport coefficients.

In writing these constitutive relations, for simplicity, we have ignored possible anisotropic transport in µ. When expanded around the isotropic equilibrium state µ = 0, such terms only contribute to the hydrodynamic equations non-linearly. More generally, we might be interested in anisotropic equilibrium states, such as magnetohydrodynamics with a constant density of magnetic fields in equilibrium, where such anisotropic contributions to the constitutive relations will become important; see [5].

Let us do a quick sanity check. As we said before, in equilibrium, the hydrodynamic fields take trivial values β µ = δ µ t /T 0 and Λ β = 0. Furthermore, all background sources must be time-independent in equilibrium, implying that
div.formula: δ B g µν = δ B A = δ B Φ = 0.(5.21)
div.p: This sets all the dissipative corrections in the constitutive relations to be identically zero and we can verify that we get the same expressions for the currents as we did using the equilibrium action in section 4.1.

div.head: Linearised analysis and mode spectrum
div.p: To understand the physical effects of these coefficients, let us switch off the background sources and take the simplified equation of state (5.15). The hydrodynamic equations are trivially satisfied by the equilibrium configuration T = T 0 , u µ = δ µ t , together with µ = µ = 0. In principle, we can also consider equilibrium states with µ = 0, but for q > 0, these states are anisotropic and would need a more careful treatment that we leave for future work. We can look at small perturbations around the said equilibrium state, which will be described by linearised hydrodynamics.
When expanding around the isotropic state with µ = 0, energy and momentum fluctuations decouple from the charge fluctuations and are simply given by
div.formula: ∂ t = -∂ i π i , ∂ t π i = -v 2 ∂ i + D ⊥ π ∂ 2 π i + D π -D ⊥ π ∂ i ∂ k π k ,(5.22)
div.p: where π i = ( + p)u i denote the momentum fluctuations and we have identified
They give rise to the familiar longitudinal fluid sound and transverse shear diffusion modes
Around a state with µ = 0, the energy-momentum sector generically non-trivially couples with the charge sector. We shall not consider this case in this work.
Coming back to the charge fluctuations, the two higherform currents take the form
div.formula: J = u ∧ n -σP u dµ, L = u ∧ n + σ µ -σ P u dµ .(5.25)
div.p: We can see that σ and σ are conductivities for U(1) q charge n and U(1) q-1 defects n respectively. On the other hand, defect current also has a term proportional to µ also controlled by σ , which tends to relax n. The conservation equations become
where we have defined the diffusion constants D n , D , and the relaxation rate Γ as
div.formula: D n = σ χ , D = σ χ , Γ = 2 σ χ .(5.27)
div.p: The first equation in eq. (5.26) determines the dynamics of U(1) q charge density n, whereas the second equation defines the defect density n . Let us go to the momentum space with frequency ω and wavevector k, and decompose the U(1) q charge density as n = k/|k| ∧ n + n ⊥ , such that ι k n = ι k n ⊥ = 0. We can see that transverse components of the U(1) q charge n ⊥ evolves independently of the defects and give rise to the usual charge diffusion mode

However, this mode is now damped due to the presence of explicit symmetry breaking. There is another damped diffusion mode associated with the fluctuations of the longitudinal components of the U(1) q charge n . This mode ise absent in the absence of defects due to the Gauss constraint n = -/k 2 n . It reads

Such a mode does not exist for q = 0. Note that defects are exactly conserved, but since they are topological and cannot propagate locally, the associated diffusion mode is still relaxed. Due to their topological nature, the damping coefficient Γ and the attenuation coefficient D in this mode are related via the damping-attenuation relation

where k 0 = χ /χ is the inverse hydrostatic correlation length of defects introduced in section 4.1. Such relations are a generic hallmark of systems with a spontaneously broken approximate symmetry, in this case the temporal part of the higher-form symmetry.

If we proliferate q-form defects by increasing the magnitude of , the relaxation rate Γ becomes finite and both the higher-form charged modes drop out of the spectrum. The resultant theory is that of a neutral fluid without any higher-form symmetry and implements the phase transition from (c) to (h) in fig. 1.
We can also use the hydrodynamic equations to obtain the retarded correlation functions of the U(1) q charge. These have been summarised in appendix C.

div.head: RELAXED HIGHER-FORM PSEUDO-SUPERFLUID DYNAMICS
div.p: In this section, we will discuss the hydrodynamic theory of a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid in the relaxed phase. For q = 0, this amounts to an ordinary U(1) 0 superfluid with charge relaxation effects. For q > 1, this describes non-equilibrium effects in a U(1) loc q-1 gauge theory in the presence of free electric charges. We will also study the effects of vortices in the hydrodynamic theory, interpreted as magnetic monopoles in the context of gauge theories.

div.head: Hydrodynamic fields and equations
div.p: We will start with the conventional formulation of (pseudo-)superfluid dynamics in terms of the Goldstone phase field φ and superfluid velocity ξ. In the later half of this subsection, we will explicitly show that this system can equivalently be posed as hydrodynamics with a temporally-spontaneously broken approximate anomalous U(1) q × U(1) p symmetry. This generalises the work of [5,7], where the authors discussed explicitly-unbroken U(1) 0 and U(1) 1 superfluids.

div.head: Conventional formulation
div.p: The hydrodynamic description of a system is based on the associated conservation equations. As we discussed at length in section 3.2, the conservation equations of a U(1) q superfluid in the relaxed phase (with vortices) take the form
div.formula: ∇ µ T µν = (F • J) ν + ( F • J) ν + ( Ξ • L) ν + ( ˜ Ξ • L) ν .(6.1e)
div.p: We have included additional Lorentz force terms in the energy-momentum conservation corresponding to the new conserved currents. In the conventional formulation of superfluid dynamics, the U(1) p conservation equation (6.1b) and the associated U(1) p-1 defect conservation equation (6.1d) are trivially satisfied due to the Bianchi identity (3.29). The respective U(1) p current J is defined in terms of the superfluid velocity ξ in eq. (3.12b), while the U(1) p-1 defect current L is given in terms of the vortex-induced part V of the superfluid phase in eq. (3.33).

To solve the remaining hydrodynamic equations, we introduce the set of hydrodynamic fields β µ , Λ β , and Λ β from section 5, with the transformation properties given in eq. (5.2). We can define the U(1) q chemical potential µ and the U(1) q-1 defect chemical potential µ same as in eq. (5.6).
Out of equilibrium, the configuration equation for φ is replaced by the respective Josephson equation determining its time-derivatives, i.e.
div.formula: £ β φ = c φ Λ β + 1 T K where ι β K = 0,(6.2a)
div.p: where K denotes arbitrary corrections to the Josephson equation that need to be determined as part of the constitutive relations. The Λ β term here is necessitated by U(1) q -invariance, rendering the derivative corrections K to be gauge-invariant. We have used the redefinition freedom in the time-component of Λ β to set ι β K = 0, keeping with our previous convention in eq. (5.4a) upon identifying ϕ = ι β φ/c φ . We can also use the redefinition freedom in Λ β as a whole to rid ourselves of K entirely, but we have other plans for this freedom later in our discussion. Since the U(1) q-1 symmetry is temporallyspontaneously broken in the relaxed phase, we also have a similar Josephson equation for pseudo-Goldstone ϕ , taking the same from as in eq. (5.4b). Analogously, in the presence of vortices, we have another Josephson equation for the vortex-induced part V of the superfluid velocity. This takes the form
div.formula: £ β V = 1 T V, where ι β V = 0,(6.2b)
div.p: with V denoting the admissible derivative corrections. In line with our gauge choice ι β V = 0 in eq. (4.21), we have also taken ι β V = 0. The consequence of having vortices is that the evolution of ξ is now dependent on both φ and V . The can be seen by expressing the two Josephson equations as
div.formula: ι β ξ = c φ µ T + 1 T K,(6.3a)
£ β ξ = c φ d µ T + ι β F + dK + 1 T ˜ V.(6.3b)
div.p: Similar to our discussion around eq. (5.5), we have a U(1) loc q-1 gauge freedom in this theory given by
div.formula: Λ β → Λ β -£ β dλ, Λ β → Λ β -£ β λ, φ → φ -c φ dλ, ϕ → ϕ -ι β λ.(6.4)
div.p: We can see that φ acts as dynamical gauge field for this U(1) loc q-1 symmetry.

div.head: Dual formulation
div.p: The set of hydrodynamic equations outlined above can also be described in terms of a temporally-spontaneously broken approximate anomalous U(1) q × U(1) p symmetry. In this formalism, we do not interpret the U(1) p conservation equation in (6.1b) as a Bianchi identity, but rather treat both U(1) q and U(1) p symmetries democratically. The hydrodynamic fields are taken to be a set of symmetry parameters Λ β , Λ β , Λβ , Λ β , and β µ , and their transformation properties are given analogous to eq. (5.2). We will take the U(1) q , U(1) p higher-form symmetries and the emergent U(1) q-1 , U(1) p-1 topological symmetries to be temporally spontaneously broken, for q > 0, q > 1, p > 0, p > 1 respectively, giving rise to the respective temporal-Goldstones ϕ, φ, ϕ , φ , with the same transformation properties analogous to eq. (5.3). Their dynamics is governed by the Josephson equations that take the same form as (5.4). This representation of the theory has two copies of gauge symmetries U(1) loc q-1 , U(1) loc p-1 analogous to eq. (5.5). However, we do not need to worry about these as long as we work with the gauge-invariant higher-form chemical potentials µ, μ, and the associated defect chemical potentials µ , μ defined similar to eq. (5.6). Together with β µ , these make up the right amount of degrees of freedom to solve for using the hydrodynamic equations (6.1).

Let us see how this description is related to the original conventional formulation of a U(1) q (pseudo-)superfluid. Essentially, we can use the constitutive relations for the temporal parts of the currents ι u J, ι u L to relate μ , μ to the spatial components of the superfluid velocity ξ and defect density dV respectively via
On the other hand, the physical information in the two Josephson equations (6.2) can be identified with the constitutive relations for the spatial parts of the currents; to wit
div.formula: P u J = - * ι u ξ = -c φ * µ + J , P u L = (-) q+1 * ι u dV = L,(6.6)
div.p: where we have defined
This ensures that the two formulations describe the same physical system.

div.head: Second law of thermodynamics and constitutive relations
div.p: Having set up the hydrodynamic model, let us construct the respective constitutive relations. Using the free energy current defined in eq. (5.9), we find that the adiabaticity equation (5.10) for a superfluid modifies to
div.formula: ∇ µ N µ = 1 2 T µν δ B g µν + J • δ B A + L • δ B Φ -K ext • δ B φ + U ext • δ B V + ∆,(6.8)
div.p: where we have defined K ext = (-) q F and U ext = Ξ. In addition to the variational definitions in eq. (5.11), we have further defined
div.formula: δ B φ = £ β φ -Λ = ι β ξ - µ T , δ B V = £ β V = ι β dV.(6.9)
div.p: We need to solve the adiabaticity equation for the constitutive relations for T µν , J, L, together with corrections to the Josephson equations K, V, in terms of u µ , T , µ, µ , ξ, dV , and the background fields, given some N µ and ∆ ≥ 0, arranged order-by-order in derivatives.

div.head: Constitutive relations
div.p: Following our discussion in section 5.2, we can set up the thermodynamics for a U(1) q superfluid. Unlike section 5.2, however, the thermodynamic pressure P can now also depend on the spatial components of the superfluid velocity ñ = (-) q * ξ and the vortex density ñ = - * dV . We can parameterise its variation as
div.formula: δP = sδT + n • δµ + n • δµ - * μ • δ * ñ - * μ • δ * ñ - 1 2 r µν dg µν , = -P + T s + µ • n + µ • n .(6.10)
div.p: This equation can be seen as defining the conjugate chemical potentials μ and μ . By using ξ as the independent degree of freedom in the conventional formulation, we are naturally led to work in the canonical ensemble with respect to ñ and ñ . We can find the associated r µν using Lorentz invariance
We can take a simplified equation of state where P only depends on the squares of the higher-form objects, leading to the thermodynamic relations
div.formula: δP = sdT + 1 2 χδµ 2 + 1 2 χ δµ 2 - 1 2 χ δñ 2 - 1 2 χ δñ 2 . (6.12a)
div.p: In this case, various densities are related to the chemical potentials as n = χµ, n = χ µ , ñ = χμ, ñ = χ μ . (6.12b)
Additionally, the anisotropic stress tensor r µν is automatically symmetric.
To analyse the adiabaticity equation, let us perform a canonical decomposition of the free energy current. We take the parametrisation
div.formula: N µ = P β µ + 1 T ( * K • μ) µ + (-) q T ( * V • μ ) µ + N µ ,(6.13)
div.p: where N µ denotes non-canonical contributions. The additional terms proportional to K and V have been included for later convenience. We are now ready to derive the constitutive relations of an ideal superfluid. Following a procedure similar to the one used for eq. (5.17), together with the variational formulae
div.formula: * μ • δ B * ñ = T (-) pq+q+1 * d μ T • δ B φ + (-) pq+p+q * μ • c φ δ B A + ˜ δ B V + c φ (-) p * (µ ∧ μ) µ u ν δ B g µν -(-) pq+q d(u ∧ μ ∧ δ B φ), * μ • δ B * ñ = T (-) pq+q+p+1 * d μ T • δ B V -(-) pq+p+q d(u ∧ μ ∧ δ B V ),(6.14)
div.p: we can find the constitutive relations
div.formula: T µν = ( + P )u µ u ν + P g µν -r µν -2c φ (-) p * (µ ∧ μ) (µ u ν) + T µν , J = u ∧ n -cφ * μ + J , L = u ∧ n + L. (6.15)
div.p: These satisfy the adiabaticity equation with ∆ = 0 when all the derivative corrections have been switched off. The total derivative terms in eq. (6.14) precisely account for the two additional terms in the free energy current decomposition in eq. (6.13).

Plugging the constitutive relations (6.15) into the adiabaticity equation together with the Josephson equations eq. ( 6.2), we can read of the constraint equation to be satisfied by the dissipative corrections
div.formula: - 1 2 T µν δ B g µν -J • δ B A -L • δ B Φ -J • δ B Ã -˜ L • δ B Φ + ∇ µ N µ = ∆ ≥ 0, (6.16)
div.p: where the variations δ B Ã and δ B Φ are defined similar to eq. (5.11). The Landau frame condition is given as
div.formula: T µν u ν = ι u J = ι u L = ι u J = ι u L = 0.

div.head: Dual formulation
div.p: The same constitutive relations can also be derived using the dual formulation in terms of anomalous higherform symmetries. The relevant free energy current is given by a canonical transformation of eq. (5.9) as
div.formula: T N µ = T N µ + ( J • μ) µ + ( L • μ ) µ = T S µ + T µν u ν + (J • µ) µ + ( J • μ) µ + (L • µ ) µ + ( L • μ ) µ . (6.18)
div.p: One can check that the respective adiabaticity equation takes a similar form to eq. (5.10) but generalised to two higher-form symmetries, i.e.
div.formula: ∇ µ N µ = N H + 1 2 T µν δ B g µν + J • δ B A + J • δ B Ã + L • δ B Φ + ˜ L • δ B Φ + ∆.(6.19)
div.p: Here δ B Ã and δ B Φ are defined similarly to eq. (5.11).
We also get a new anomaly-induced "Hall free energy" contribution in the adiabaticity equation given by
Similarly, the canonical decomposition of the free energy current in eq. (6.13) becomes
div.formula: N µ = P β µ -(-) p c φ T * (µ ∧ μ) µ + N µ ,(6.21)
div.p: where the conjugate pressure is given as

U(1) p charges, through the linear term in the chemical potentials, eventually giving rise to the damping-attenuation relations like eq. (5.30). However, interestingly, we now also have cross coefficients γ × , γ × , γ× , γ × coupling the U(1) q and U(1) p symmetries. The physical significance of these will be clear below. In the anisotropic case, we expect more intricate couplings between various currents, leading to many more transport coefficients.

Requiring the microscopic theory underlying the hydrodynamic description to be invariant under time-reversal transformations, the primed and unprimed cross coefficients are related via the Onsager's relations

leaving only two of the cross coefficients to be independently physical. With the Onsager's relations in place, we find that the cross coefficients do now contribute to the entropy production quadratic form ∆ obtained through eq. (6.16). Demanding entropy production to be positive semi-definite, we find the inequality constraints on the remaining six coefficients η, ζ, σ, σ, σ , σ ≥ 0. (6.29)
We will discuss the physical implications of these coefficients in the next subsection.
For completeness, let us also record the corrections to the φ and V Josephson equations obtained using eq. (6.7), leading to
div.formula: T K = (-) pq+q σ * T d μ T + ι u F + (-) pq+p+q γ × T P u d µ T + (ι u Ξ -µ) , T V = (-) pq+p+q σ * T d μ T + ˜ ι u Ξ -μ -γ × T P u d µ T + ι u F .(6.30)
div.p: In particular, notice that there is a term proportional to µ in the constitutive relations for K coming with the coefficient γ × . This will modify the leading order c φ µ term in the Josephson equation (6.3a) to λc φ µ, where λ = 1 -γ × /c φ . Similarly, there is a term proportional to μ = (-) q * ξ/ χ in the constitutive relations for V coming with the coefficient σ . Plugging this into the equation of motion (6.3b), this term will relax the spatial components of ξ with relaxation rate Γ = ˜ 2 σ / χ. More on this in a moment.

We have carefully parametrised the constitutive relations, so that the theory is self-dual under the exchange of tilde and untilde quantities. This implements the U(1) q ↔ U(1) p duality of the relaxed phase of a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid with vortices.

div.head: Linearised analysis and mode spectrum
div.p: To understand the physical meaning of the hydrodynamic equations and various transport coefficients, let us perform the same simplifications as in section 5.3. In particular, we focus on fluctuations around an equilibrium state with zero densities, so the energy-momentum fluctuations decouple and lead to the same longitudinal sound and shear diffusion modes in eq. (5.24). The higher-form charge currents, on the other hand, take the form (6.31) where We see that the λ coefficient modifies the leading order Josephson equation and screens the effective chemical potential as seen by the superfluid phase. The same result was also found for 0-form superfluids in [8], with the coefficient γ × /c φ being identified as -σ × /σ φ from that paper. For q > 0, this coefficient also controls the coupling of the Josephson relation to the U(1) q defect chemical potential. The coefficient σ, on the other hand, causes the Goldstone to diffuse and can be identified with 1/σ φ from [8].
div.formula: J = u ∧ n -λc φ * μ -σP u dµ -(-) pq+p+q γ× * dμ , J = u ∧ ñ -λc φ * µ -σP u dμ -(-) pq+p+q γ × * dµ , L = u ∧ n + σ µ -σ P u dµ + (-) p γ × * dμ, L = u ∧ ñ + ˜ σ μ -σ P u dμ + (-) q γ× * dµ,
div.p: Using the constitutive relations, we can see that the two higher-form conservation equations become
div.formula: (∂ t + Γ)n = (-) p λ s χ cφ * dñ + D n ∂ 2 n -(-) pq+p+q (D -D n )d * dn, (∂ t + Γ)ñ = (-) q λ s χ c φ * dn + Dn ∂ 2 ñ -(-) pq+p+q ( D -Dn )d * dñ,
div.p: where we have defined two copies of damping and attenuation coefficients as in eq. (5.27). We have also defined a combined renormalisation factor

For completeness, let us also write down the conservation equations in terms of the superfluid velocity ξ, leading to Let us decompose the U(1) q and U(1) p charge densities into longitudinal and transverse components according to n = k/|k| ∧ n + n ⊥ and ñ = k/|k| ∧ ñ + ñ⊥ . In the same spirit, decomposing the spatial components of the superfluid velocity,
div.formula: (∂ t + Γ)n = (-) pq λ s c φ f s * d * ξ + D n ∂ 2 n -(
P u ξ = k/|k| ∧ ξ + ξ ⊥ , we can see that ñ⊥ = (-) q |k| * (k ∧ ξ ), ñ = -1 |k| * (k ∧ ξ ⊥ ). (6.37)
div.p: If it were not for the λ s coupling terms in eq. ( 6.34), the transverse components of higher-form densities n ⊥ and ñ⊥ (or the longitudinal components of the superfluid velocity ξ ), would lead to two independent damped diffusion modes like eq. (5.28). However, because of the coupling terms, these combine into the dispersion relations
div.formula: iω -D n k 2 -Γ iω -Dn k 2 -Γ + v 2
div.p: ⊥ k 2 = 0, (6.38) where we have defined the transverse sound speed v ⊥ as
If we take , ˜ ∼ k, i.e. assume both the defect-induced relaxation Γ and the vortex-induced relaxation Γ to be O(k 2 ), this yields a second sound mode characteristic of superfluids
However, the sound mode is damped due to presence of defects and vortices.

If we proliferate vortices by taking ˜ ∼ O(1), thereby making Γ ∼ O(k 0 ), the dispersion relations instead give rise to a charge diffusion mode weakly damped due to defects and a strongly damped mode

The resultant mode spectrum is precisely that of a U(1) q (pseudo)-fluid discussed in section 5.3. Depending on the presence of explicit breaking of the U(1) q symmetry, this implements the phase transition from (f) to (a) or (g) to (c) in fig. 1. Instead, if we proliferate U(1) q defects by taking ∼ O(1), thereby making Γ ∼ O(k 0 ), the charge diffusion mode gets strongly relaxed and we instead get a weakly damped vortex diffusion mode
This is a signature of a U(1) p (pseudo-)fluid and implements the phase transition from (d)= (f) q↔p to (a) q↔p or (g) q↔p to (c) q↔p depending on the presence of vorticess.

Finally, fluctuations in the longitudinal components of the higher-form densities n and ñ (or the transverse components of the superfluid velocity ξ ⊥ ), we find two defect diffusion modes mediated by n and ñ respectively
div.formula: ω = -iΓ -iD k 2 , ω = -i Γ -i D k 2 . (6.43)
div.p: These modes would be absent in the absence of defects and vortices respectively. These modes will also be absent for q = 0 and p = 0 respectively.

div.head: PINNED HIGHER-FORM PSEUDO-SUPERFLUID DYNAMICS
div.p: In this section, we discuss the hydrodynamics of a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid in the pinned phase. For q = 0, this describes a pinned U(1) 0 superfluid. 15

div.head: Hydrodynamic equations
div.p: Following our discussion in section 3.3, the conservation equations for a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid in the pinned phase are given by
div.formula: d J = c φ F + (-) q+1 L,(7.1a
div.p: d L = (-) q c φ Fψ , (7.1c)
div.formula: d J = cφ F,(7.1d
∇ µ T µν = (F • J) ν + ( F • J) ν + ( Ξ • L) ν + ( Fψ • Jψ ) ν . (7.1e)
div.p: In the conventional formulation of a pseudo-superfluid (gauge theory), the U(1) ψ p+1 conservation equation (7.1b) and the U(1) p conservation equation (7.1d) are identically satisfied due to the Bianchi identities associated with the misalignment tensor ψ and the superfluid velocity ξ. The definitions of the respective currents are given in eqs. (3.46) and (3.12b). For the remaining hydrodynamic equations, we have the set of hydrodynamic fields β µ , Λ β , and Λ β from section 5, with the transformation properties given in eq. (5.2). These can be used to define the U(1) q chemical potential µ and the U(1) q-1 defect chemical potential µ same as in eq. (5.6).

The Josephson equation for the U(1) q Goldstone φ also takes the same for as in eq. (6.2a). In the pinned phase, there is a similar Josephson equation associated with the U(1) q-1 Goldstone φ , taking the form
div.formula: £ β φ = c φ Λ β + 1 T K where ι β K = 0. (7.2)
div.p: Similar to our discussion around eq. ( 6.2a), we have used the redefinition freedom in the temporal components of Λ β to absorb derivative corrections in the temporal direction. The Josephson equation (5.4b) for the temporal-Goldstone field ϕ = ι β φ /c φ follows as the time-component of the equation above. Recast in terms of gauge-invariant variables, the two Josephson equations lead to the relations
div.formula: ι β ξ = c φ µ T + 1 T K, ι β ψ = -c φ µ T - T K . (7.3a)
div.p: Similar to our discussion around eq. (5.5), we also have a U(1) loc q-1 gauge freedom in this theory
div.formula: Λ β → Λ β -£ β dλ, Λ β → Λ β -£ β λ, φ → φ -c φ dλ, φ → φ -c φ λ.(7.4)
div.p: The field φ acts as the dynamical gauge field associated with the U(1) loc q-1 gauge symmetry, while the field φ can be thought of as the Goldstone arising from its spontaneous breaking. Following usual lore of Higgs mechanism, the dynamical gauge field φ eats the Goldstone φ to acquire a mass, precisely giving rise to the gauge-invariant misalignment tensor ψ.

div.head: Dual formulation
div.p: We can also view the pinned phase of a U(1) q pseudosuperfluid from the perspective of hydrodynamics with a temporally-spontaneously broken approximate anomalous U(1) q × U(1) ψ p+1 global symmetry. The relevant hydrodynamic variables are a set of symmetry parameters Λ β , Λ β , Λψ β , Λβ , and β µ , and their transformation properties are given analogous to eq. (5.2). We will take all the higher-form symmetries to be temporally-spontaneously broken, giving rise to the respective temporal Goldstone fields ϕ, ϕ , φ, and φψ , with transformation properties similar to (5.3). We can use these to define U(1) q and U(1) q-1 chemical potentials same as eq. (5.6b). On the other hand, we have two more chemical potentials for U(1) p and U(1) ψ p+1 symmetries, given as
div.formula: μψ T = Λψ β + ι β Ãψ -d φψ ,(7.5a)
μ T = Λβ + ι β Ã -d φ -φψ . (7.5b)
div.p: Note that the definition of μ needs to be modified to make it invariant under the new U(1) ψ p+1 transformations exclusive to the pinned phase. The temporal Goldstone fields satisfy Josephson equations similar to eq. (5.4). These imply that all the chemical potentials are purely spatial, satisfying constraints similar to eq. (5.7).

To relate this formalism to the conventional formulation, we can notice that the constitutive relations for the temporal parts of the currents ι u J, ι u Jψ to relate μ , μψ to the spatial components of the superfluid velocity ξ and misalignment tensor ψ respectively via
On the other hand, the two Josephson equations (7.3) can be seen as determining the constitutive relations for the spatial parts of the currents; to wit
div.formula: P u J = - * ι u ξ = -c φ * µ + J , P u Jψ = (-) q * ι u ψ = (-) q+1 c φ * µ + Jψ ,(7.7)
div.p: where we have defined
This ensures that both the formulations describe the same pinned phase of a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid.

div.head: Second law of thermodynamics and constitutive relations
div.p: Using the free energy current defined in eq. (5.9), we find that the adiabaticity equation (5.10) for a superfluid modifies to
div.formula: ∇ µ N µ = 1 2 T µν δ B g µν + J • δ B A + L • δ B Φ -K ext • δ B φ -Q ext • δ B φ + ∆.(7.9)
div.p: where we have defined Fψ = -Q ext . The variations of the phase fields are defined similar to eq. (6.9).

div.head: Conventional formulation
div.p: Thermodynamics in the pinned phase can be defined similar to eq. (6.10). However, the thermodynamic pressure P does not depend on the (p -1)-form vortex chemical potential μ anymore and instead depends on the misalignment tensor ñψ = * ψ. We find
div.formula: δP = sδT + n • δµ + n • δµ - * μ • δ * ñ - * μ ψ • δ * ñ ψ - 1 2 r µν dg µν , = -P + T s + µ • n + µ • n .(7.10)
div.p: This equation can be seen as defining the conjugate chemical potentials μ and μ . We can find the associated anisotropic stress tensor r µν using Lorentz invariance
If we take a simplified equation of state where P only depends on the squares of the higher-form objects, the thermodynamic relations become
div.formula: δP = sdT + 1 2 χδµ 2 + 1 2 χ δµ 2 - 1 2 χ δñ 2 - m 2 2 δñ 2 ψ .(7.12a)
div.p: In this case, various densities are related to the chemical potentials as n = χµ, n = χ µ , ñ = χμ, ñψ = 1 m 2 μψ . (7.12b) As before, the anisotropic stress tensor r µν is automatically symmetric.
The free energy current can be decomposed in the canonical parametrisation similar to eq. (6.13) as
div.formula: N µ = P β µ + 1 T ( * K• μ) µ + (-) q T ( * K • μψ ) µ +N µ ,(7.13)
div.p: where N µ denotes non-canonical contributions. Using the variational formulae
div.formula: * μ ψ • δ B * ñ ψ = -(-) s T * d μψ T • δ B φ + (-) pq+q * μ ψ • (δ B φ -c φ δ B Φ) -c φ (-) p+q * (µ ∧ μψ ) (µ u ν) δ B g µν -(-) s d(u ∧ μψ ∧ δ B φ ),(7.14)
div.p: we can derive the constitutive relations for an ideal U(1) q pseudo-superfluid in the pinned phase
div.formula: T µν = ( + P )u µ u ν + P g µν -r µν -2c φ (-) p * µ ∧ μ -(-) q µ ∧ μψ (µ u ν) + T µν , J = u ∧ n -cφ * μ + J , L = u ∧ n + (-) p cφ * μ ψ + L,(7.15)
div.p: These satisfy the adiabaticity equation with ∆ = 0 when all the derivative corrections have been switched off. Note the additional terms compared to constitutive relations in the relaxed phase given in eq. (6.15). These drop out if we take μψ = m 2 ñψ to zero, i.e. turn off the mass m of the pseudo-Goldstone fields.

Plugging the constitutive relations (7.15) into the adiabaticity equation together with the Josephson equations eq. ( 7.3), we can read of the constraint equation to be satisfied by the dissipative corrections
div.formula: - 1 2 T µν δ B g µν -J • δ B A -L • δ B Φ -J • δ B Ã -Jψ • δ B Ãψ + ∇ µ N µ = ∆ ≥ 0,(7.16)
div.p: where we have defined
div.formula: δ B Ã = d μ T + ι β F - μψ T , δ B Ãψ = d μψ T + ι β Fψ .(7.17)
div.p: The Landau frame condition for various dissipative corrections is given as
div.formula: T µν u ν = ι u J = ι u L = ι u J = ι u Jψ = 0.(7.18) 7.2

div.head: .2. Dual formulation
div.p: We can also formulate U(1) q pseudo-superfluid in the pinned phase using the language of anomalous approximate U(1) q ×U(1) ψ p+1 symmetry. The relevant free energy current can be defined similar to eq. (6.18) as
div.formula: T N µ = T N µ + ( J • μ) µ + ( Jψ • μψ ) µ = T S µ + T µν u ν + (J • µ) µ + ( J • μ) µ + (L • µ ) µ + ( Jψ • μψ ) µ .(7.19)
div.p: One can check that the respective adiabaticity equation takes a similar form to eq. (5.10) but generalised to two higher-form symmetries, i.e.
div.formula: ∇ µ N µ = N H + 1 2 T µν δ B g µν + J • δ B A + J • δ B Ã + L • δ B Φ + Jψ • δ B Ãψ + ∆. (7.20)
div.p: Here δ B Ã and δ B Ãψ are defined similarly to eq. (5.11).
We also get a new anomaly-induced "Hall free energy" contribution in the adiabaticity equation given by
div.formula: N H = - c φ T (-) pq+q µ • F + (-) q μ • F + (-) pq+p+q µ • Fψ -µ ψ • Ξ . (7.21)
div.p: Similarly, the canonical decomposition of the free energy current in eq. (7.13) becomes
div.formula: N µ = P β µ -(-) p c φ T * (µ ∧ μ -(-) q µ ∧ μψ ) µ + N µ ,(7.22
div.p: ) where the conjugate pressure is given as
div.formula: P = P + ñ • μ + ñψ • μψ . (7.23)
div.p: The differential of the new pressure yields the thermodynamic relations
div.formula: δP = sδT + n • δµ + ñ • δ μ + n • δµ + ñψ • δ μψ - 1 2 r µν δg µν , = -P + T s + µ • n + μ • ñ + µ • n + μψ • ñψ ,(7.24)
div.p: together with
Using these, together with the identifications in eq. ( 7.7), we can read off the constitutive relations in the dual formulation
div.formula: T µν = ( + P )u µ u ν + P g µν -r µν -2c φ (-) p * µ ∧ μ -(-) q µ ∧ μψ (µ u ν) + T µν , J = u ∧ n -cφ * μ + J , J = u ∧ ñ -c φ * µ + J , L = u ∧ n + (-) p cφ * μ ψ + L, Jψ = u ∧ ñψ -(-) q c φ * µ + Jψ .(7.26)
div.p: Note that the definitions of the dissipative corrections J and Jψ coincide with eq. (7.8). Ignoring all derivative corrections, we can see that we have arrived at exactly the same form of the constitutive relations as derived using an equilibrium action in section 4.2. The constraint equation for dissipative corrections in the higher-form formulation takes the same form given in eq. (7.16).

div.head: Dissipative corrections
div.p: Similar to our discussion in section 6.2.3, we will assume the constitutive relations to be parity and chargeconjugation invariant and ignore all anisotropic contributions. The constitutive relations for T µν and J take the same form as in the temporally-spontaneously broken phase in eq. (5.20). The constitutive relations for J and L are the same in form as the relaxed phase in eq. (6.27), but there are new μψ contributions coming from the modified definition of δ B Ã in eq. (7.17), i.e.

div.head: J = -T σ P
div.formula: u δ B Ã -(-) pq+p+q T γ × * δ B Φ = -σ T P u d μ T + ι u F -μψ -(-) pq+p+q γ × * T d µ T + (ι u Ξ -µ) , L = -T σ P u δ B Φ + (-) p T γ × * δ B Ã = -σ T P u d µ T + (ι u Ξ -µ) + (-) p γ × * T d μ T + ι u F -μψ .(7.27)
div.p: Since μψ = m 2 ñψ = m 2 * ψ contains a term linear in φ, the σ contribution to J is now responsible is now responsible for relaxation of φ with relaxation rate Ω = 2 m 2 σ. This plays a role very similar to the vorticityinduced relaxation rate Γ observed in the relaxed phase, but is qualitatively distinct. The μψ contribution coupled to γ × in L renormalises the pinning term in eq. (7.26). We need to supply new dissipative constitutive relations for Jψ , given by
div.formula: Jψ = -T σψ P u δ B Ãψ = -σ ψ T P u d μψ T + ι u Fψ .(7.28)
div.p: Note that this object does not exist for q = 0. In the context of U (1) loc q-1 gauge theories, the coefficient σψ modifies the relation between the scalar gauge potential ι β φ and the electric chemical potential µ , and will result in damping of the vector gauge potential P u φ.

One can check that that the dissipative corrections map to each other under the q ↔ p + 1 self-duality transformation of the pinned phase, provided that we transform the dissipative transport coefficients as follows
In particular, note that the conductivities σ and σ do not map to each other unlike the duality transformations in the relaxed phase.

div.head: Linearised analysis and mode spectrum
div.p: We will again focus on fluctuations around an equilibrium state with zero densities, so the energy-momentum fluctuations decouple and lead to the same longitudinal sound and shear diffusion modes in eq. (5.24). The constitutive relations for the higher-form currents in the absence of background fields take the form
div.formula: J = u ∧ n -cφ * μ -σP u dµ, J = u ∧ ñ -λc φ * µ + σ μψ -σP u dμ -(-) pq+p+q γ × * dµ , L = u ∧ n + (-) p λc φ * μ ψ + σ µ -σ P u dµ + (-) p γ × * dμ, Jψ = u ∧ ñψ -(-) q c φ * µ -σψ P u dμ ψ .(7.30)
div.p: The constitutive relations for J and Jψ give rise to the Josephson equations
div.formula: ι u ξ = λc φ µ -2 m 2 σφ + (-) pq σ χ * d * ξ + (-) pq+p+q γ × dµ , ι u φ = -c φ µ -(-) pq+p+q m 2 σψ * d * φ.(7.31)
div.p: We have specialised to a gauge where φ = 0, meaning that ψ = φ in the absence of background fields. Compared to the relaxed phase expressions in eq. ( 6.33), ι u ξ has a new pinning term proportional to φ controlled by 2 m 2 . The second Josephson equation, which is specific to the pinned phase and is only non-trivial for q > 1, provided the relation between the scalar potential ι u φ and the defect chemical potential µ .
Let us look at the linearised equations of motion. In the higher-form language, they are given as
div.formula: (∂ t + Γ)n = (-) p λ χ cφ * dñ -(-) p λm 2 cφ * ñ ψ + D n ∂ 2 n -(-) pq+p+q (D -D n )d * d * n, (∂ t + Ω)ñ ψ = - λ χ c φ * dn + λ χ c φ * n + Dψ ∂ 2 ñψ + (-) pq ( Dn -Dψ )d * d * ñ ψ , * d * n = (-) pq+p+q+1 n , * d * ñ ψ = (-) pq ñ ,(7.32)
div.p: where we have defined the diffusion and relaxation coefficients

In terms of the conventional formulation variables, we instead find In particular, note that phase field φ is now directly relaxed with the characteristic rate Ω, unlike the relaxed pseudo-superfluid phase in the presence of vortices, where the superfluid velocity is relaxed instead with rate Γ.
div.formula: (∂ t + Γ)n = (-) pq λ χ c φ * d * ξ + D n ∂ 2 n + (D -D n )dn -2 λm 2 c φ φ, (∂ t + Ω)φ = - λ χ c φ dn + Dψ ∂ 2 φ +(
div.p: To obtain the respective mode spectrum, let us first look at the transverse U(1) q density sector, spanned by n ⊥ and n ψ , we find the dispersion relations
div.formula: iω -D n k 2 -Γ iω -Dn k 2 -Ω +ω 2 0 +v 2 ⊥ k 2 = 0, (7.
div.p: 35) where we have identified the pinning frequency
Taking ∼ k, this leads to a pinned and damped sound mode
In the longitudinal U(1) q density channel, spanned by n and ñ⊥ ψ , we find another set of dispersion relations
div.formula: iω -D k 2 -Γ iω -Dψ k 2 -Ω + ω 2 0 + v 2 k 2 = 0,(7.38
div.p: ) where we have defined
This leads to another damped and pinned sound mode
The two sets of modes map to each other under q ↔ p + 1 self-duality, which maps
div.formula: χ ↔ 1 m 2 , χ ↔ χ, v ⊥ ↔ v , k 0 ↔ k φ 0 , D n ↔ D ψ , D ↔ Dn , Γ ↔ Ω. (7.41)
div.p: and leaves λ and ω 0 invariant.

If we increase the strength of explicit symmetry breaking by increasing the magnitude of , both the relaxation rates Γ and Ω will become finite, thereby gapping our both the sets of modes above and implementing the phase transition from the pinned superfluid phase to the neutral fluid phase. This is the transition from (e) to (h) in fig. 1.

div.head: OUTLOOK
div.p: In this paper we have introduced a new framework to classify phases of matter according to their approximate higher-form symmetries. In particular, we focused on phases with a U(1) q generalised global symmetry and various patterns of symmetry breaking therein at zero and finite temperature. These patterns include temporal-(pseudo)-spontaneous symmetry breaking in higher-form fluids and complete-(pseudo)-spontaneous symmetry breaking in higher-form superfluids. We showed how these phases naturally incorporate dynamical q-form defects and p-form vortices, where p = d -1 -q. In all such phases of matter, we studied their out-of-equilibrium dynamics by formulating appropriate hydrodynamic theories that respect the symmetry breaking patterns. As we commented throughout the paper, this framework is applicable to many phases of matter including smectic crystals with topological defects, superfluids with vortices, polarised plasmas with free electromagnetic charges, magnetohydrodynamics with magnetic monopoles, spin ices, superconductors, among many others.

One of our main interests in this work was to understand potential phase transitions that can occur by the proliferation of q-form defects or p-form vortices. As we mentioned earlier, these transitions include the melting transition and the plasma phase transition. In order to address the physics of these transitions in a hydrodynamic framework, in sections 5-7 we studied the linearised spectrum of various phases. We noted that in what we denoted by relaxed phases (or Coulomb phases) the higherform charge is relaxed but the Goldstone is not, while in pinned phases (or Higgs phases) both the higher-form charge and the Golstone field is relaxed. By carefully studying the linearised spectrum of excitations in various regimes we could clearly identify features of phase transitions by proliferating defects or vortices. In particular, upon proliferation, certain modes become gapped and the spectrum of q-form (pseudo)-fluids or uncharged fluids is recovered.

It should be noted, however, that our linearised mode analysis was not the most general one. We focused on isotropic phases by linearly perturbing around a state with vanishing chemical potential. While this is most relevant equilibrium state in certain contexts such as in 0-form superfluids, it is not the most physically relevant state in other contexts as in the case of smectic crystals or in magnetohydrodynamics with constant magnetic fields. The various correlation functions derived in appendix C reflect the same choice of equilibrium states. As the main purpose of this work was to provide a new framework for describing phases of matter with higher-form symmetries, we have not performed an exhaustive analysis of the phenomenology of the mode spectra and correlators in the context of specific systems. In an upcoming series of papers, we will be using the formalism introduced here to study the dynamics of specific phases of matter.

Our work has dealt with relativistic field theories, which is the natural language to describe electromagnetism and magnetohydrodynamics but also to describe the effects of elasticity in cosmological matter [27]. However, many of the relevant applications of approximate higher-form symmetries are to non-relativistic systems, including spin ices and superconductors. Following the approach of [8,9] it is straightforward, though cumbersome, to couple these field theories to non-relativistic backgrounds. We plan on discussing this elsewhere. At the same time, having formulated this framework in the context of relativistic theories makes this work ideal to study phases of matter using holography. A few holographic studies of higherform symmetries have been performed (see e.g. [6,15,16,[28][29][30][31]) and it appears to be possible to extend them to approximate U(1) q symmetries more generally.

Finally, we would like to note that we focused on continuous U(1) higher-form symmetries. Given the broad scope of applications of this single case we studied here, it would be extremely interesting to study other (discrete) higher-form symmetries and more exotic symmetries such as higher-group symmetries, subsystem symmetries and non-invertible symmetries in the context of hydrodynamics and phase transitions.

Gauge anomalies in quantum field theories can be classified using the anomaly inflow mechanism. Herein, one posits that an anomalous (d + 1)-dimensional system, described by a symmetry-non-invariant effective action S, can be coupled to a (d + 2)-dimensional bulk with a symmetry-non-invariant action S bulk , so that the full theory described by
div.formula: S tot = S bulk + S, (B.1)
div.p: is symmetry-invariant. The bulk action S bulk has to be such that the it is gauge-invariant up to a boundary term. It must also be a topological theory so that there are no propagating degrees of freedom residing in the bulk. In light of these requirements, anomalies can be characterised by an exact (d + 3)-form anomaly polynomial made out of the wedge-product of gauge field strengths. The bulk Lagrangian is then given by a Chern-Simons form I CS related to the anomaly polynomial as P = dI CS , so that
div.formula: S bulk = bulk I CS . (B.2)
div.p: The explicit form of the anomaly polynomial depends on the dimensionality of the spacetime and the symmetry group under consideration. For a system with an anomalous U(1) q × U(1) p higher-form symmetry, where p + q = d -1, the anomaly polynomial is given by P = (-) q F ∧ F .
The anomaly polynomial results in the Chern-Simons form for the bulk Lagrangian
div.formula: I CS = F ∧ Ã. (B.4)
div.p: We have chosen the bulk Lagrangian to respect the U(1) q part of the symmetry and violate U(1) p , but we can also choose the conventions to be the other way round.
We can parametrise the variations of the boundary part of the action S as
div.formula: δS = δA ∧ J cons + δ Ã ∧ J + δΦ ∧ L + ˜ δ Φ ∧ L. (B.5)
div.p: Note that we have used the conserved current J cons , also often called the "consistent current" in the anomaly literature. Since we have chosen to manifest the U(1) q symmetry in eq. (B.4), the associated current J cons obtained by varying the boundary part of the action S will be exactly conserved, but will not be gauge-invariant; see eq. (3.9). Whereas, the U(1) p current J will be gaugeinvariant but anomalous; see eq. (3.12). We have allowed for symmetry violation terms in line with our discussion in the main text. Depending on the application in mind, they can be switched off by turning of or ˜ accordingly. We can vary the total action S tot with respect to the background fields to yield δS tot = bulk (-) q δA ∧ F + (-1) pq+q δ Ã ∧ F
div.formula: + δA ∧ J + δ Ã ∧ J + δΦ ∧ L + ˜ δ Φ ∧ L, (B.6)
div.p: where the boundary variations of the total action define the "covariant current" J as
div.formula: J = J cons + (-) pq+p+q Ã. (B.7)
div.p: Since the full theory is meant to be invariant under both U(1) q and U(1) p symmetries, we are led to the conservation laws given in eq. (3.26), (3.34), or (3.37), depending on the structure of explicit symmetry breaking.

Let us note the form of the bulk action in thermal equilibrium in the context of our discussion in section 4.2. Using the definitions of the higher-form chemical poten-tials form (4.4), we can see that
div.formula: S eqb bulk = Σ bulk β ι u I CS = Σ bulk β (-1) q µ ∧ F + (-1) pq+q μ ∧ F - Σ β T 0 F ∧ φ -µ ∧ Ã. (B.8)
div.p: The bulk part in this expression is completely gaugeinvariant. Therefore, in equilibrium, the anomaly in the respective quantum field theory can be generated by the boundary part of the bulk action, which is precisely what we need to cancel the anomaly-induced terms in eq. (4.29).
The construction for the U(1) q × U(1) ψ p+1 anomaly in the pinned phase of a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid follows similarly. The anomaly polynomial is given as
div.formula: P = (-) q F ∧ F -Ξ ∧ Fψ , (B.9)
div.p: where we have used the U(1) ψ p+1 -invariant definition of F from eq. (3.42). The relative coefficient between the two terms can be fixed by requiring that P is closed. The anomaly polynomial leads to the Chern-Simons form
div.formula: I CS = F ∧ Ã + (-) q Ξ ∧ Ãψ . (B.10)
div.p: We have, again, chosen the bulk Lagrangian to be invariant under U(1) q part of the symmetry group.

In this case, we can parametrise the variations of the boundary action S in terms of the "consistent currents" according to Finally, we record the form of the bulk action in thermal equilibrium. In this instance, we find The conservation equations and constitutive relations in the U(1) p sector follow similarly by performing a tildeconjugation.

div.p: and similarly for the tilde-conjugates.

Let us now look at the explicit correlation functions. The transverse density sector correlation functions in eq. note.p: We use the notation ξµν and φµ for the electromagnetic field strength and gauge field instead of the conventional Fµν and Aµ because, as we shall discuss further, the polarised plasma phase of electromagnetism can be understood as a 1-form superfluid with the 1-form φµ playing the role of the associated Goldstone and ξµν its "superfluid velocity".

Note that this does not necessarily mean that melting in arbitrary spatial dimensions is guided by the proliferation of vortices. While this is known to be true in 2 spatial dimensions, in arbitrary dimensions this just means that proliferation of vortices would contribute to melting but might not be the primary mechanism as we increase temperature.

For q = 0, no such damping-attenuation relation exists for relaxed 0-form pseudo-fluids; see fig.1(c). This is because the U(1) 0 symmetry in this phase is only explicitly broken and not spontaneously broken. However for q > 0, this phase features partial-spontaneous breaking of the U(1)q symmetry in the timedirection, yielding the damping-attenuation relations.
APPROXIMATE HIGHER-FORM SYMMETRIES AT ZERO TEMPERATURE
Some aspects of explicit symmetry breaking of higher-form symmetries were discussed in specific models in the context of holography[15,16].
We discussed 1-form superfluids in a previous paper[5], where we chose the charge of the Goldstone to be c φ = 1. In the work of[7], the authors used c φ = -a.
The action(3.4) only strictly respects the U(1)p if the spacetime is unbounded. This is because the U(1)p and U(1)q symmetries have a mixed anomaly, as we discuss below.

For q = 0 case, we can actually redefine the background phase Φ → Φ -α /c φ to make it invariant under "U(1) -1 " transformations in eq. (2.9). The misalignment tensor is then just given by ψ = (φ -Φ) as in our previous work[8].

In recent years, a new action principle approach for non-equilibrium thermal systems has been formulated, known as the Schwinger-Keldysh effective field theory[17][18][19]; see[20] for a review. Among other things, this framework requires a doubling of all degrees of freedom and a new KMS symmetry. At the level of classical hydrodynamic equations, this framework is equivalent to the conventional framework of dissipative hydrodynamics which we explore in this paper.Here µ 0 is the equilibrium value of the chemical potential, χ the susceptibility, and ι β denotes the exterior product with respect to β µ . The reason why this works is because the time-component of the gauge field ι β A is invariant under time-independent gauge transformations. Unfortunately, as we can see from eq. (4.1), this is no longer the case for q = 0. In fact

It is still possible to construct higher-derivative theories with spontaneously unbroken higher-form global symmetry, with terms such as dι β A ∧ dι β A . However, the resultant q-form density is zero in the absence of background fields. See[5].
The quantity -T 0 S eqb can be identified with the offshell free energy of the system.
In coordinates, this integral is justΣ β * f = T -1 0 d d x f .

div.head: Dissipative corrections
div.p: For simplicity, let us assume the constitutive relations to be parity and charge-conjugation invariant. The discrete transformation properties of various objects are given in table I. We also ignore any all anisotropic dissipative transport. With these in mind, the constitutive relations for the dissipative energy-momentum tensor T µν take precisely the same form as before in eq. (5.20). Next, we have transport in the higher-form currents. It turns out that the dissipative (q +1)-form flux J and the dissipative p-form defect flux L mutually couple, give rise to
We see the same coupling structure in the dissipative (p + 1)-form flux J and the dissipative q-form defect flux L, taking the form

The coefficient σ, σ are the conductivities of U(1) q , U(1) p charges respectively, which we met already in section 5. Similarly, σ , σ are conductivities of the respective defects. They are also responsible for the relaxation of U(1) q ,
Appendix A: Differential form conventions
In this appendix we give details about our differential form conventions. A q-rank differential form or q-form µ can be expressed in components as
The standard operations: Hodge dual, exterior derivative, interior product, and interior derivative

Here µ1...µ d+1 is the (d + 1)-rank totally antisymmetric Levi-Civita tensor, with 012... = √ ±g. The upper sign corresponds to Euclidean spacetime while the lower one to Lorentzian spacetime. Also, X µ is a vector field. Given a q-form µ and another r-rank form ν, we can define their exterior-product or wedge-product as

We also introduce another notation for contraction of differential forms. We define (µ • ν) µ1...µr-q = 1 q! µ ν1...νq ν ν1...νqµ1...µr-q , q ≤ r, (µ • ν) µ1...µq-r = 1 r! µ µ1...µq-rν1...νr ν ν1...νr , q ≥ r.

A special differential form is the spacetime volume-form vol d+1 = 1. It can be used to integrate a function f (x) over spacetime

which is understood to be zero everywhere in spacetime other than the hypersurface. This can be used to integrate a q-form J can be integrated as

div.head: Appendix B: Anomaly inflow for higher-form symmetries
div.p: In this appendix, we discuss the anomaly inflow mechanism for U(1) q × U(1) p and U(1) q × U(1) ψ p+1 mixed anomalies used in this work. We will set the anomaly coefficient c φ = 1 in this appendix for clarity. The anomaly coefficient can be restored by multiplying all the relevant expressions with c φ .

div.head: Appendix C: Retarded correlation functions
div.p: In this appendix, we compute the retarded correlation functions of various higher-form densities and fluxes using our hydrodynamic model.

div.head: Approximate higher-form fluids
div.p: Let us start with the U(1) q fluid with an approximate temporally-spontaneously broken U(1) q global symmetry. Split into longitudinal and transverse sectors, the higherform conservation equations looks like
Note that the longitudinal defect density n is identically zero. The constitutive relations take the form

We can use the constitutive relations together with the conservation equations to find the classical expectation values of the (approximately) conserved densities and fluxes in terms of the background fields.

This procedure can be used to compute the retarded correlation functions of hydrodynamic densities and fluxes. Denoting operators by O and respective sources by J, the retarded correlation functions are defined as
If the microscopic description underlying the effective theory is invariant under time-reversal symmetry, the correlation functions satisfy the so-called Onsager reciprocal relations
where η T are the time-reversal eigenvalues of various operators given in table I.

Let us start with the transverse density sector, spanned by the transverse density n ⊥ , longitudinal flux j , and transverse defect flux j ⊥ . There are three independent retarded correlation functions in this sector, given as

The correlation functions involving the defect flux j can be obtained using the Ward identities (C.1). In the longitudinal density sector, spanned by the longitudinal density n , transverse flux j ⊥ , defect density n ⊥ , and the longitudinal defect flux j . We find density and flux correlators

Note that G R n j ⊥ is trivially zero by isotropy. The defect density and flux correlators can be read off trivially from here using the Gauss constraints in (C.1). Note that if we turn off explicit symmetry breaking, the longitudinal charge density n identically vanishes and so does its correlator.

div.head: Higher-form pseudo-superfluids: relaxed phase
div.p: For a U(1) q superfluid, the discussion is slightly more involved. Since the U(1) q symmetry is now anomalous, the conservation equations (C.1) become
where
is the Hodge duality operation transverse to the wavevector and fluid
It is trivial to see that all the mixed correlators vanish if we turn off the anomaly coefficient c φ .

div.head: Higher-form pseudo-superfluids: pinned phase
div.p: We finally look at the correlation functions of a U(1) q pseudo-superfluid in the pinned phase. The conservation equations in the U(1) q sector modify from eq. (C.7) to include the new Ãψ source terms
The associated constitutive relations take the form
The conservation equations and constitutive relations in the U(1) p sector can be obtained by performing the q ↔ p + 1 self-duality transformation of the pinned phase.
The correlators in the transverse U(1) q density sector in the pinned phase modify from their relaxed phase expressions in eq. (C.10) to

We see that these expressions are almost identical to the relaxed phase correlators in the presence of vortices (magnetic monopoles) in eq. (C.10), except that the vortex-induced relaxation Γ gets exchanged by pinning-induced relaxation Ω and the pole has an additional ω 2 0 pinning term.
